data.title: Alphabet Q2: That's Why I Keep The Company In My Recommended Portfolio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my recommendation to Buy Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL, NASDAQ:GOOG) shares after the release of its Q2 2025 results. This article is a continuation of my introductory coverage article published on December 12, 2024, with the same recommendation.

The company released its results on July 23, 2025, and they exceeded market expectations, as we'll see throughout this article. Amidst the many challenges facing AI and user behavior, this article aims to present Alphabet's results, along with opinions and perspectives.

Net Revenue - Growth In All Segments

Net revenue rose 12% YoY to $96.43 billion, while estimates had pointed to $94.04 billion. Growth was seen across all businesses, including Search Advertising, which grew +12% YoY, driven by engagement with search features like AI Overviews, which already has 2 billion monthly users.

Other growth drivers include YouTube Ads (+13% YoY) and Google Play (+20 % YoY). However, the fastest-growing segment is still Google Cloud, which reached $13.6 billion (+32% YoY).

My assessment of this segment of the results is very positive. Gemini usage increased 35x YoY with the adoption of AI workflows for corporate clients, and as for outlook, CEO Pinchai stated that he expects further growth in the user base. CFO Ashkenazi indicated a possible positive impact from exchange rates in 3Q25, which is consistent with my thesis of dollar depreciation.

Operating Profit - Impressive Jump In Cloud

The company reported a 14% YoY increase in operating profit to $31.27 billion, equivalent to a margin of 32.4%. When analyzing business lines, we see a contribution from Google Services, but a significant contribution from Google Cloud given its size compared to other segments.

Google Cloud's results are so impressive that its operating margin jumped from 11.3% in Q2 24 to 20.7% in Q2 25. While Google Services maintained its operating margin at 40%, which I consider quite adequate.

My outlook for this segment is also positive, based on comments from company executives. Management indicated in the Q&A that the focus is on balancing AI talent acquisition with efficiency.

CapEx - The Point Of Attention

The main point of note in the results is the updated investment outlook. The company now estimates investing $85 billion in 2025 (previously $75 billion). CEO Pinchai stated that the focus will be on expanding into AI, and that investments in 2026 are expected to be even higher.

As an analyst, I must say that the numbers are quite frightening. However, I follow the line of my articles about the company, in which I praise the quality of its management. In fact, Warren Buffett frequently talks about the importance of management in his investments, and I believe that Alphabet's management deserves the benefit of the doubt in this case.

Net Income

Finally, the company reported a 19% YoY increase in net income to $28.2 billion. This represents earnings per share of $2.31, above market expectations of $2.20.

My assessment of the company's results is very positive. The company exceeded analysts' expectations, reporting double-digit growth in net revenue and net income. Management's plans seem both promising and diligent. The figure below provides a general summary of the results, and then we move on to the valuation.

Valuation

For the valuation, I used a DCF model with a 5-year projection and terminal value based on perpetual growth. Projections include 12% revenue growth in 2025, gradually decelerating through 2029.

Additionally, the EBITDA margin expands from 37% to 49.9%, reflecting the operational efficiency gains that will come from AI. Speaking of AI, CapEx is projected to include $85 billion in 2025 and $90 billion in 2026, as indicated by management. More assumptions are below.

The model indicates a fair value of $223.31 per share, a 17.4% upside compared to the current price. Therefore, I reiterate my Buy recommendation for Alphabet shares based on the strong results reported and the attractive valuation, according to my assumptions.

I emphasize that the recommendation is consistent with my recommended stock portfolio for 2025, with Alphabet among its holdings. Year to date, the portfolio is up 13.8%, while the SPY ETF is up 8.36%.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Without a doubt, the main risk to this thesis is the failure to realize returns on investments, especially if the AI and Cloud segments don't grow at the expected pace. It's worth noting that supply chain challenges pose an additional adversity at this point.

Still on the topic of AI, I see monetizing AI products as another challenge for the company. If the user experience isn't well-designed, intense competition could lead to a decline in user engagement. Finally, I believe that retaining AI talent will be vital to the company's success.

Among other issues, legal settlement expenses are another risk to monitor, especially with antitrust and regulatory issues. The thesis is quite complex, and investors should analyze it carefully.

The Bottom Line

Alphabet reported strong results, exceeding analyst expectations and posting double-digit growth in net revenue and net income. Google Cloud was a standout, with its operating margin nearly doubling year over year.

The announcement of an increase in CapEx from $75 billion to $85 billion by 2025 did not scare investors this time. Incorporating this data and other assumptions into the DCF model, we have a 17% upside, which seems attractive to me for this thesis.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my Buy recommendation for Alphabet shares. As with other articles, I must emphasize the importance of investors trusting the company's management and its investment approach.

data.title: Netflix: More Challenges As Streaming Competition Heats Up
data.description:
Netflix's (NASDAQ:NFLX) Q2 results may have energized some, despite a few small negatives:

Revenue of $11.08 billion, and beating the forecast on earnings with EPS of $7.19.

But the US streaming market is saturated, with 96% of 124 million total available households. There's hardly any exponential growth left with global room for growth only. Netflix hard charge ahead to focus on global markets and pricing power moves in the US and Canada. But now as peers are playing better catch-up ball, the gaps in total customers will begin to narrow between the top 10 streamers.

We have now entered a final phase of growth where the average household already has at least four services. So, exponential growth in a great part of the US will slow down. And Netflix, whose focus on volume and margin, will need to change course to maintain its runaway leadership of the sector. But can it? I think not. Not because it fails with managing the task. It by far is one of the best managed companies in the field on every measure.

Netflix will depart the exponential trading range it now holds to decline significantly while saturation globally continues and peers gain share of market each month as they introduce new content.

This means Netflix shareholders now need to adjust its expected trading range for the future price disparity of the stock, which will narrow considerably. It will descend into a regular price range close to its peers. We have seen this "price democracy" form in many sectors over time. So, expectations for investors need to be curbed.

If you've been in Netflix since the early 2000s, and now breaking a big smile at well over $1,200, start to think about a watchful hold. If you are in lower, think about selling off a chunk and putting it into the "cash is king" account. If you're really up since the stock traded under $300, run for the hills and figure a buyback strategy if you smell weakness as you review the free cash flow underperformance.

The point is this: Do you believe Netflix is vastly overbought now or do you believe there are gallons upon gallons of gas left in the tank?

Netflix overbought, overpriced: Time for a good hard look

The truth: Netflix is a brilliantly conceived business model difficult to pick flaws in if you're building a bear case because you're a happy holder. Thus far, current competitors have proven themselves incapable of coming close. Peers are catching up as the universe of total streaming customers expands globally. Yet the US market has little unexploited territory to offer challengers. So what we will see is Netflix and its peers trading like utilities together in a sector perhaps three years from now. Growth in subs will be a tough ticket for newbies. Part of the reason: The 96% saturation. Expect this to begin waning next year.

Of course, we recognize the different lanes in which Netflix runs and the pure play advantage it may have in the heads of many investors who don't have to consider the main business performance of the parent. Simplicity is the heart of the Netflix model. It was the same when it challenged Blockbuster with mail exchange of red envelopes by mail.

It was the most logical switch of highways for Netflix from the built-in US postal service to the TV screen. It was an incredible decision to use what was there rather than reinvent the wheel by creating a network. Yet a part of a simple system of clicking a handful of buttons on your TV was to open a vast world of entertainment at your fingertips suited to fit snugly in your lifestyle.

Then build a price list within the affordability of everyone and slowly raise prices to compensate for rising production costs. And heavily promote features and exclusive films. But most critically, think of your service as the marketing of an Amazon by streaming the process free of bumps, obstacles, and odd thinking and giving you time to mull your choices, which are at least 14.

The trends are beginning: Runaway prices on Netflix shock trading in prices way beyond peers due to the early mover experience, transforming to sustained market share dominance from peers. Phase two: Runaway shares begin to reach excessive heights and share of market begins to fall. At the same time, peers begin trending higher, bringing a balanced price for all.

The era of towering disparity in pricing evens out. We move into a utility stock phase within a few years. Investors now need to shave down their hope for appreciation ahead to something more realistic. with history of past unicorns.

Netflix: $1,180

Disney (DIS): $122 (conglomerate but trades up on streaming growth)

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD): $12.85

Paramount (PARA) :$13.26

Comcast (CMCSA): $35.754

Above: The streaming sector is diverse between pure play Netflix and key peers with diverse entertainment giants with footprints in movies, TV productions, live entertainment, TV network shows, etc. While the analogy isn't perfect between a pure-play stock and one that's part of a conglomerate, that's how they compete. They all present the exact same product, similar promotions, and state taxes with few exceptions. Prepare yourself with lowered expectations than when you opened your first position.

Conclusion

We think the big runs for Netflix are over and that the next growth phase will spread across the sector entirely as subscriber growth widens for peers. Netflix has to find it harder to poach shares from awakened peers.

Our view: Time to take a dollar-for-dollar audit of your gains and decide whether to sit tight or begin to unload chunks of stock safely in the bag.

data.title: CGI Q3 Preview: U.S. Federal Exposure Is A Huge Risk, Initiate With Sell Rating
data.description:
CGI Inc. (NYSE:GIB) is a Canadian based IT consulting company with 94,000 consultants globally. I believe the company is more likely to face growth challenges due to its 13%+ revenue exposure to the U.S. federal government. I am initiating with a Sell rating with a fair value of CAD $109 per share. or US$79 per share.

As an IT consulting company, CGI relies on both organic expansion and acquisitions to grow its topline. As detailed in the table below, the company has delivered an average of 4.4% constant revenue growth over the past 8 years. Its total bookings declined by 1.3% in FY24 due to a weak IT consulting market.

As illustrated in the charts below, the U.S. commercial and state government represented around 16% of total bookings. As noted in its annual report, CGI generated around 13.6% of total revenue from the U.S. federal government including its various agencies in FY24.

The government sector is the largest market for CGI, accounting for around 40% of total bookings. As reported by the media, the U.S. government has been cutting its IT consulting projects, and terminating several discretionary IT consulting projects. The U.S. government crackdown on consulting has already caused significant challenges to IT consulting companies. For instance, IBM (IBM) reported its Q2 FY25 result on July 23, delivering flat consulting revenue growth in the first half of FY25. During its earnings call, IBM’s management expressed concerns about the overall consulting market demands. As such, I anticipate CGI will face significant growth headwinds from the U.S. government sector.

In addition, due to the U.S.-Canada tariff disputes, I believe the Canadian economy will deteriorate in the near future, posing further challenges for overall IT consulting markets, across both public and private sectors.

In recent quarters, CGI has been restructurings its Western and Southern Europe business. I think CGI lacks the brand awareness and scale to complete with global consulting companies in Europe, which resulted in 2.7% revenue decline in FY24. I think its current restructuring activities demonstrate a setback for its "Build and Buy" strategy in Europe. In March 2025, CGI completed its acquisition of Novatec, an IT consulting firm servicing Germany and Spain. While the deal could strengthen CGI’s market presence in Europe, I think CGI remains a very small player there, and Europe is unlikely to generate any meaningful growth for them in the near future.

CGI released its Q2 FY25 result on April 30th, posting 3.3% constant revenue growth and 7.6% adj. EPS growth, as detailed in the slide below:

During the earnings call, the management disclosed that 2% of its U.S. Federal revenues were derived from discrete consulting projects, which are more likely to be cut in FY25. The management also highlighted that the U.S. government was actively seeking cost saving opportunities, and CGI has engaged in conversations with these agencies to help them reduce spendings. While 50% of CGI’s contracts with federal government are outcome based, I think CGI will face near-term growth challenges from its federal government exposures.

On its balance sheet, the company exited the quarter with more than CAD $1.1 billion in cash and investments, and CAD $4.36 billion in total debt, with a 24.1% net debt to capitalization ratio. As the company generated more than CAD $2 billion in free cash flow in FY24, I think CGI has a strong balance sheet to support is growth and acquisition strategy.

As usual, CGI does not provide any guidance. I anticipate CGI will deliver a low-single-digit constant revenue growth for the upcoming quarter, reflecting the weakness in Federal government market growth, when it is expected to report earnings on July 30th. For FY25, I forecast the company will deliver 4% total revenue growth, representing 3% organic growth, 2% M&A growth and 1% headwinds because of weak USD currency. I anticipate its organic revenue growth in FY25 is lower than its normalized organic growth of 4.5%, because of its U.S. federal government exposures. From FY26 onwards, I anticipate its business will begin to normalize and deliver 6.2% total revenue growth, assuming 4.5% organic revenue growth and 1.7% growth from M&A. I project CGI will allocate 4% of revenue towards M&A, contributing to an additional 1.7% growth to the topline.

I forecast the company will deliver 20bps annual margin expansion, driven by its ongoing restructuring activities. I calculate its total operating expenses will grow by 5.9% annually, resulting in a 17.8% of operating margin by FY34.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate the FCFF as follows:

I set the WACC to be 9.9% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.1; equity risk premium 6%; equity C$28 billion; debt C$4.36 billion; tax rate 25%. I estimate the terminal growth rate to be 4.5%, aligns with overall consulting market growth.

Discounting all the future FCFF, the fair value is calculated to be CAD $109 per share, or US $79 per share, assuming 1 CAD equals 0.73 USD (CAD:USD).

Upside Risks

As I assign a Sell rating, I am considering the potential upside catalysts for CGI’s stock. CGI has repurchased more than C$1.7 billion in its own shares over the past two years, thanks to its strong cash generations. The company repurchased another $498 million in Q1 FY25. I anticipate CGI will continue to repurchase its shares, which could provide some technical support for its stock price.

In addition, CGI has experienced strong early-renewal activities among its banking customers, focusing on IT system modernization, as noted in its earnings call. Financial services accounted for almost 20% of total revenue in FY24. As such, a strong growth in the financial institution market could potentially offset its weakness in the federal government market.

Lastly, CGI could potentially deliver a better operating margin than expected in the upcoming quarter, due to its relentless cost cutting initiatives. If the company delivers a strong margin, the market might pay less attention to its weak topline growth.

Conclusion

As an IT consulting company, CGI is facing significant growth challenges from its U.S. Federal government exposures. In my view, the Canadian economy is at risk of deterioration due to the U.S.-Canada tariff disputes, which could further hurt CGI’s growth. I am initiating with a Sell rating with a fair value of CAD $109 per share or US$79 per share.

data.title: Pool Corporation: Getting Caught In Macroeconomic Turbulence After Resilient Q2
data.description:
Pool Corporation (NASDAQ:POOL) reported the company’s Q2 results on the 24th of July in pre-market hours. The swimming pool product distributor reported fairly good results from the summer season, but lowered the 2025 profit guidance to reflect continued industry weakness. Macroeconomic factors underline a challenging environment for Pool Corp.

I initiated Pool Corp at a Sell rating in my September 2024 article, titled “Pool Corporation: Too Optimistic Growth Is Priced In”. Since, the valuation has somewhat moderated, as Pool Corp’s stock has lost -10% of its value, while the S&P 500 has gained 11%.

Pool Corp Reported a Resilient Q2, But a Weaker 2025 Outlook

Pool Corp’s Q2 results came in fairly close to expectations. Revenues reached $1.78 billion for the seasonally strong summer season quarter, nearly in line with Wall Street’s consensus. The EPS of $5.17 beat Wall Street’s consensus estimate by a small $0.06 margin; on the surface level, financials came in as could be expected.

The report still has several factors to consider. Notably, the quarter returned Pool Corp to positive year-on-year growth – as I outlined in my previous article, Pool Corp has been experiencing a post-Covid normalization in demand, which previously showed up as nine consecutive quarters of year-on-year sales declines. The Q2 report now showed slow but positive revenue growth at a total of 0.8%, reflecting improved discretionary spending and growth in maintenance product sales as Pool Corp finally looks to stabilize. Europe even showed very strong 7% growth in sales for Pool Corp, while some states in the U.S. showed severe weakness as sales declined by -3% in California and by -2% in Texas.

The quarter does still reflect subdued industry activity as the slow growth significantly trails Pool Corp’s long-term growth, for example standing at a 2015-2024 revenue CAGR of 9.4%. With Pool Corp’s continued sales center expansion, growing by six year-on-year to a total of 451, the slow topline expansion isn’t a particularly good growth level. Highlighting such subdued industry conditions, pool product retailer Leslie’s (LESL) has continued to post very weak sales related partially to weak weather. Considering the macroeconomic backdrop, where consumer confidence remains low as tariffs have caused economic volatility from April forward and housing starts remain very subdued in the U.S., lowering demand for construction materials due to persisting high mortgage rates and other factors that I have outlined in a recent article, Pool Corp’s sales performance was very solid.

Regardless of a turbulent industry backdrop, Pool Corp also posted consistent profitability in Q2. The gross margin stayed stable at 30.0% as Pool Corp has managed to mitigate tariffs through pricing, and with prudent operating cost management, operating income came in at $273 million at a stable 15.3% margin.

Notably, while second quarter results were fairly good when considering the economic environment, Pool Corp lowered the company’s 2025 guidance – macroeconomic factors seem to be catching up to Pool Corp. The company now expects an EPS of $10.8-11.3 for the year instead of $11.1-11.6 previously as Pool Corp seems to expect economic volatility to continue – Q2 could have been boosted by extraordinarily warm weather during Spring and heatwaves in June, but underlying macroeconomic conditions have stayed weaker, causing Pool Corp to suggest flattish sales for the entirety of 2025. Weak new housing starts seem to be causing lower construction and renovation-related sales than were previously expected.

In total, I believe that the Q2 report was fair. Pool Corp understandably sees pressure from macroeconomic turbulence, weakening 2025 expectations, but the reported results were resilient. The company seems to have good pricing power regarding tariffs, and good cost control has stabilized profitability at a good level. The stock’s slightly positive reaction is warranted in my opinion.

Updated Valuation: Pool Corp Remains Too Expensive

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Pool Corp, reflecting more subdued industry conditions. I estimate revenues to remain flat in 2025, but that growth accelerates afterwards closer to Pool Corp’s long-term level. I now assume a 2024-2034 CAGR of 5.6% as a fair base scenario.

Pool Corp’s long-term growth has brought in great operating leverage, and I ultimately expect the trend to return as growth improves. I now assume Pool Corp to eventually reach a 14.0% EBIT margin through gradual margin expansion. The after-tax cash flow conversion should again stand good going forward.

The estimates put Pool Corp’s fair value at $250.84, 24% below the stock price at the time of writing – I still believe Pool Corp to be overvalued. At a forward P/E of 30.1, significant earnings growth is already priced in, making the risk-to-reward weak especially as the current outlook is stained by macroeconomic uncertainty.

The fair value estimate compares to $250.22 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a low 3.97% interest rate, annualized from Pool Corp’s Q2 interest expenses and debt. I still assume quite a modest amount of debt at a 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.42% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance now estimates Pool Corp’s beta at 1.10. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 9.70% and the WACC at 9.09%.

Takeaway

Pool Corp reported fair Q2 results in a turbulent industry backdrop, underlining resilient earnings and a return to positive sales growth. At the same time, the weak macroeconomic backdrop is catching up to Pool Corp as the company had to lower the 2025 guidance. As Pool Corp’s stock remains too expensive with my base scenario expectations, I remain with a Sell rating.

data.title: NXJ: This Fund Offers New Jersey Retirees Double Tax Exemption
data.description:
Overview

Nuveen New Jersey Quality Municipal Income Fund (NYSE:NXJ) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive exposure to municipal investments that are exempt from federal and New Jersey state income taxes. Since the fund is primarily invested in NJ issued bonds, residents of NJ can add a fund like this to a diversified portfolio as a way to reduce their tax liability. The fund has an inception dating back to 2001, which means we have over two decades worth of performance history to reference. Additionally, NXJ has a reasonable management fee of 0.58%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, NXJ's share price has declined by about 11.3%. I believe that these declines can be attributed to the fund's heavy use of leverage. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return still sits at a loss of about 4%. The lower share price translates to NXJ now offering investors a high starting dividend yield of about 8.5%. The most appealing aspect to a fund like NXJ is the ability to collect a high income that is tax-exempt.

Despite the poor performance, I believe that the fund currently offers an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The fund can be an efficient choice for retired investors that are looking for a place to park a large sum of cash. The current dividend yield is attractive enough to produce a sizeable stream of income and the tax-efficient nature of the fund makes this a great choice for investors that want to live off their dividend income. I believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a positive growth catalyst for the fund, so entry here may be attractive. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NXJ implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy & Risk

According to the fact sheet, NXJ has total managed assets of $824M that is spread across approximately 330 different holdings. The fund's primary objective is to provide current income that is exempt from regular federal and New Jersey income taxes. The fund achieves this by investing in municipal securities and specifically focuses on investing at least 80% of its assets into securities that are rated investment grade. The fund aims to invest in under valued securities and capitalize on this mispricing.

The fund primarily chooses to invest in different Tax Obligation/Limited securities, which accounts for 31.1% of total assets. These are most commonly sourced from local counties, school districts, or cities and are coming from predictable tax revenues, such as property taxes. The 'limited' aspect of these investments simply means that the issuer must repay bondholders but can only raise taxes up to a certain legal limit. This is followed by exposure to Transportation and Education investments, which account for 18.1% and 12.3% of assets respectively.

So when looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that the New Jersey Transportation Trust Fund Authority is one of the largest positions. These investments are tied to things like highways, public transportation, and other related infrastructure. We can also see that Union County and the Township of North Brunswick are larger positions, which indicates that these municipalities may have strong credit profiles.

The fund aims to invest the majority of its assets into securities with stronger credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating of BBB and above are classified as investment grade. These are typically borrowers that present a much lower risk profile because they are more likely to repay any loans. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • US Guaranteed: 1.2%
  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 88.8%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - B): 3.2%
  • Not Rated: 6.9%

Sticking with investment grade borrowers is ideal since the fund utilizes an aggressive amount of leverage in an attempt to amplify returns. The fund currently uses leverage equivalent to 42.84% of assets, which amounts to $382.5M. This is a very aggressive amount of leverage during this period of elevated interest rates. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated. A higher interest rate means that the cost of debt is elevated. NXJ's annualized average cost of leverage currently sits around 3.64%. A higher interest rate environment can mean that the spread between NXJ's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes smaller.

Just to help visualize how important leverage and interest rates are for a fund like NXJ, I pulled in the chart below to demonstrate the inverse relationship. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels due to the pandemic, NXJ's price rapidly increased to the upside. A lower cost of debt meant that NXJ's leverage was more effective and it provided an environment that catered to growth. Conversely, NXJ's price rapidly declined as interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Since rates remain elevated, NXJ's price hasn't been able to recover back to its prior levels.

Therefore, there's always the risk that interest rates remain elevated and NXJ's price continues to trade sideways or decline. With uncertainty around how tariffs will impact markets, the Fed continues to leave interest rates unchanged. The Fed continues to monitor metrics like the unemployment rate, inflationary data, and consumer spending shifts and have concluded that interest rate cuts don't make sense at this time. However, I anticipate that interest rate cuts may happen within the next twelve months, which can serve as a positive growth catalyst.

Valuation

Since NXJ operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, NXJ currently trades at a small discount to NAV of about 8%. However, NXJ has traded at an average discount to NAV closer to 11.7% over the last three year period. Therefore, the current discount level may seem unattractive at first glance. However, I believe that the discount to NAV has only become smaller due to the combination of a declining price and higher distributions.

In order to get better clarity around the fund's performance so far, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.20 per share, while NXJ experienced net realized losses of $0.11 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income levels have been much higher in prior years when interest rates were lower. For instance, net investment income was as high as $0.71 per share in 2021. As previously mentioned, the lower net investment income can be attributed to a lower spread between NXJ's cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities. I anticipate that the net investment income performance will improve when interest rates are lower.

Similarly, the fund hasn't been able to generate positive net realized gains on a consistent basis. Net realized losses occur when the underlying bonds or securities are sold for less than they're purchased for. The consistent losses here can be attributed to a higher interest rate environment, the impact of leverage, and the demand for higher yields during periods of uncertainty. As a result of these losses, the fund hasn't been able to maintain any positive NAV growth. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $16.44 per share
  • 2022: $15.49 per share
  • 2023: $13.36 per share
  • 2024: $13.89 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $13.29 per share.

Despite these issues, I strongly believe that NXJ's primary issue is the large amount of leverage that is being used. Once interest rates are lower, this should create a more favorable environment for funds like NXJ. I think that patient investors will be able to capitalize on these lower valuations and benefit from a price recovery as investor confidence in the debt market improves when rates are lower.

Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0785 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.5%. This high starting yield makes NXJ an attractive choice for investors that want to prioritize dividend income within their portfolio. However, I do anticipate that NXJ may slightly reduce its dividend at some point due to the inconsistent performance. If we multiply the current payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.942 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, here are the earnings over the last few years:

  • 2021: ($0.01) per share
  • 2022: ($0.25) per share
  • 2023: ($1.57) per share
  • 2024: $0.91 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.09 per share

As we can see, NXJ hasn't actually generated a sufficient amount of earnings that can outpace the distribution amount for five consecutive years now. Since NXJ is paying out more than it earns, the NAV and share price continue to decline. Therefore, a distribution cut is essential at this point in order to preserve the health of the NAV. However, I believe that management increased the payouts towards the end of 2024 as a way to reduce the fund's discount to NAV valuation.

Since NXJ holds municipal bonds that collect interest income that's exempt from federal income tax, shareholders should expect to receive distributions that are not federally taxed. If you are a NJ resident, the distributions from NXJ are also exempt from NJ state taxes. This double tax exemption benefit is the primary benefit for shareholders and is why a NJ may decide to buy into the fund despite its inconsistent performance. If you are an out-of-state investor, you would still be able to enjoy federally tax exempt income, but not the state exempt benefit.

The latest section19(a) notice reveals that the fund heavily leans on return of capital to fuel distributions. This makes sense since the fund isn't currently generating enough income to support payouts. Unfortunately, I would consider this type of return of capital to be harmful since it is actively contributing to NAV erosion over time. However, an unintended benefit may be the fact that return of capital distributions help reduce an investor's cost basis over time.

Takeaway

Despite its flaws and underwhelming performance in recent years, I do believe that NXJ can be a compelling buy at its current valuation. The heavy use of leverage has contributed to much of the underperformance and I believe that future interest rate cuts will serve as a positive catalyst for growth. Lower interest rates would improve the spread between NXJ's cost of debt and the yield collected from underlying securities. Additionally, the fund's tax benefits make the fund an enticing choice for NJ residents that want to limit their tax liabilities. This can be a useful tool for retired investors that are currently living off the dividend income within their portfolio.

data.title: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company Remains One Of The Most Compelling Prospects In The Market
data.description:
We don't know exactly when yet, but likely early next month, The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company (NASDAQ:GT) will report financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. I'm very interested in what the company will be reporting. After all, it's one of the few companies that I own shares in at this time. In fact, across my three portfolios, it accounts for 10.9% of all of my assets. I also have another 0.6% of my portfolio tied up in call options related to the company. The business has so far been pretty good to me. I started buying the stock in August 2024. In total, I'm up 23.1% on my common units. On my options, I'm up 27%. But in this environment, things can change rather rapidly.

This is especially true for a company that's going through a major transformation like this one is. Management has been selling off assets and working to reduce overall leverage. They are cutting costs and have seen some great progress on that front. I maintain that upside from here should be significant. But of course, there are certain things that investors should be paying attention to when earnings do come out that will give us a good understanding of what the future holds. For now, I'm staying bullish, maintaining the company as a "Strong Buy" candidate.

The tire keeps rolling

Back in early April of this year, I decided to revisit The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company. In that article, I talked about the resilience of the company in light of the global trade war situation that had started at that time. Even though the broader market declined, shares of the business closed up 11.7% on April 3. I touched briefly in that article on results from the last three fiscal years. But most of the emphasis of the article was on cost cutting initiatives and the impact that tariffs would have on the enterprise. My general conclusion was that while there was uncertainty regarding future tire demand because of the deleterious effects that tariffs would have on car purchases, the tariffs could also prove very positive because, for years, cheap imported tires had been eating the company's lunch.

The only new fundamental data that we have since then covers the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business came in at $4.25 billion. That was down from the $4.54 billion the business reported one year earlier. In the America's, the company saw a 3.3% drop in sales from $2.59 billion to $2.50 billion. This was the result of a 3.1% drop in tire units from 19 million to 18.4 million. Management blamed this on increased competitiveness in the United States from the lower tier tire market and the transitory impact of distribution changes that the business saw throughout Latin America. The pain was particularly visible on the consumer business side of things.

In total, lower tire volumes negatively affected revenue to the tune of $65 million. But there were other factors to take into consideration also. Primarily, foreign currency fluctuations hit revenue for that unit by $60 million because of the weakening of the Brazilian real and the Mexican peso. The good news is that increased chemical sales boosted revenue by $30 million. And the company also saw a $13 million benefit from its massive restructuring program known as Goodyear Forward.

Similar weakness was visible when it came to the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) part of the company. Revenue dropped 5.2% from $1.35 billion to $1.28 billion. This was driven in part by a 2% drop in tire units from 12.5 million to 12.3 million. Increased competition on the consumer side of things more than offset market share gains associated with new fitments. An asset sale negatively affected revenues for this business by $12 million. Lower volumes affected revenue by $23 million, while foreign currency fluctuations hit revenue by $72 million. Fortunately, the company benefited from higher prices and a change in product mix. Those helped boost sales by $43 million.

Even more problematic was the Asia Pacific part of the business. Revenue plunged 21.3% from $602 million to $474 million. Overall tire units dropped from 8.9 million to 7.8 million. That drop, which amounted to 12.4% year over year, was largely centered around replacement tire volume that dropped as the company moved away from lower margin operations and experienced softness in the consumer replacement category. Overall, lower volume negatively affected revenue here by $68 million. The sale of its OTR business hit the firm by $38 million. Foreign currency fluctuations hit revenue by $8 million, while changes in pricing and product mix took another $8 million toll.

Even though revenue for the company fell, profitability actually improved. Net income went from negative $57 million to $115 million. However, this is a bit deceptive. While there were some legitimate improvements, such as a decline in interest expense from $126 million to $115 million, most of the benefit actually came from a $262 million gain on the sale of assets that the company booked. Rationalization charges associated with the firm's transformation initiatives offset this to some extent, rising from $22 million last year to $81 million this year. Other profitability metrics were undeniably negative. Operating cash flow worsened from negative $451 million to negative $538 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $151 million to $87 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company pulled back from $387 million to $334 million.

With figures like this, you might be perplexed as to why I'm so bullish about the company. As I mentioned at the outset of this article, management continues to make progress where it matters most. This is a long-term play based on changes being made at the corporate level. Those can be painful in the near term but stand the opportunity to be very profitable in the long run. For instance, management is still expecting to achieve about $1.5 billion in cost actions and margin expansion as part of its Goodyear Forward plan that was launched in 2023.

For this year alone, at least through the first quarter of the year, the company achieved $200 million of annualized cost savings of the $750 million it has been targeting. Most of this progress is being made on the footprint and plant optimization side of things, though some of it involves purchasing, certain operational costs, supply chain and research and development initiatives, and the push toward higher margin offerings as the company moves away from lower margin products. The current plan is for annualized run rate savings to come out to $1.50 billion by the end of this year. This follows $480 million of segment operating income benefits achieved throughout 2024.

The latest big move that the company made here that was publicized was in May when management announced the sale of its Chemical business. This involved the sale of chemical facilities in parts of Texas, as well as a research office in my own city of Akron, Ohio. In total, this deal will bring the company $650 million in cash. But investors will probably have to wait until later this year for that to be finalized. Earlier that same month, the company completed the sale of its Dunlop brand that it had previously announced. This brought in $735 million to the company, with $526 million consisting of the purchase price, $105 million serving as a support fee that the firm was paid related to intellectual property and customers of that business, and $104 million worth of inventory. Management expects this to result in a pre-tax gain of $395 million for the second quarter of this year. So certainly, earnings will be distorted to some extent.

When it became clear that The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company had decided to go through a major transformation initiative, I detailed in an article what this would mean for the future of the business from a cash flow perspective. In the table above, I recreated this analysis using more updated figures and relying on the assumption that management's long-term view of the company will remain unchanged. They have not said anything that would indicate otherwise, though with economic conditions weakening and global tire volumes down 2% in the second quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, we could see a bit of lumpiness.

As the aforementioned table shows, there is the opportunity for The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company to generate significant cash flows, especially if it continues with its plan to allocate the proceeds from its asset sales toward debt reduction. To understand what kind of upside potential shares might have from where they are now, I need only point you to the chart above. This shows upside of between 169.6% and 706.1%. This should be based on the assumption that The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company should trade at the same multiples as competitor Bridgestone (OTCPK:BRDCY) (OTCPK:BRDCF).

However, that probably is a bit aggressive, at least from a price to adjusted operating cash flow perspective. After all, my model assumes that management hits its targets of between 2 and 2.50 for its net leverage ratio. By comparison, Bridgestone currently has a net leverage ratio of just 0.12, so its overall risk profile is lower. Even if we assume that the picture is far worse than this, it's not difficult to see how investors could experience a doubling in share price as management's cost cutting plans continue to come to fruition.

Of course, investors should pay attention to new data when it comes out early next month. Outside of headline financial metrics, I would argue that updates to the firm's plan, and the impact that tariffs can be expected to have on the demand and cost environment for its products would be the most important things for shareholders to take into consideration. When it comes to the financial metrics, as the table below illustrates, analysts believe that the overall financial picture for the company will worsen. But again, some of that has to do with asset sales, while the rest can be attributed to foreign currency fluctuations and weakness in the firms end markets. Parsing out how much of any weakness that is reported can be attributed to temporary things or structural changes will also be very important.

Takeaway

As a value investor who loves margin of safety, I believe that The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company makes for an appealing prospect right now. I have a long history of buying into companies that are going through major changes like this and doing quite well with them. Examples in the past have been AT&T (T), the General Electric family of companies, and Facebook parent Meta Platforms (META) when it was experiencing significant pessimism because of some of its investment decisions. I firmly believe that The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company will also experience nice upside like all of these did as well. Because of this, I have no problem keeping the company rated a "Strong Buy" for now.
